data.title: Coupang: The Real Growth Potential Could Be In Front
data.description:
E-commerce platform Coupang Inc. (NYSE:CPNG) has delivered a strong performance in 2025, but the real growth may be further down the line.

An introduction to Coupang

Coupang Inc. is an e-commerce platform founded in South Korea in 2021 but now headquartered in Seattle, Washington. The company is often labeled the 'Amazon of South Korea' and has expanded into food delivery, fintech, and video streaming. The company operates more than 100 global fulfillment centers with a large fulfillment and logistics workforce, which is increasing in automation.

Coupang showing strength in weakened markets

Coupang Inc. has seen its share price surge by around 45% year-to-date, and a recent price boost came from its second quarter earnings release on August 5. Takeaways from the earnings release were a 16% year-over-year revenue increase to $8.5 billion and stronger margins, with a nearly 230 basis points to 32.6%.

The company is developing a strong footprint in Taiwan with quarterly revenue growth of 23% in Q4, surging to a 54% quarterly gain in the current quarter. Year-over-year revenue growth reached triple digits in Q2, with management expecting a further improvement in Q3.

The initial point I would make on the Buy recommendation is that Coupang has been operating in Asian markets with an economic headwind. China's recovery from the lockdown era has been slower than expected and can continue to pick up steam in the months and years ahead.

"Our investments in customer experience continue to drive deeper customer adoption and engagement, fueling the durable revenue growth at high multiples of the relatively flat Korean retail market," CEO and founder Bom Kim said in the recent earnings call.

Seeking Alpha noted that tech-focused hedge fund Whale Rock Asset Management bought 1.41M shares in Q2.

Automation, AI, and cloud driving growth

Coupang has embraced an operating system that is heavily reliant on technology with an AI-driven management system that runs from customer order to delivery. The company employs robotics for stacking and moving products around its fulfillment centers.

Bom Kim added in the latest earnings call that AI is already core to operations and will be a "long-term enabler of both top line growth and margin expansion."

Coupang is also at the early stages of potential growth in its cloud offering. The company has used cloud to enhance its e-commerce platform, but recent government contracts have analysts looking at expansion. The current GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) business segment could expand as AI use cases grow.

One area that Coupang specializes in is bringing overseas brands to its markets, rather than the opposite. A recent success was California's POM entering the Korean market via the platform. POM Arils was awarded 'Best New Product' in the Coupang marketplace and appeals to health-conscious Asian consumers. Coupang can leverage that opportunity as it expands its presence in markets such as Taiwan.

A deeper look at earnings and valuation

The response to the Q2 earnings was positive on revenue growth but weighed down by a drop in free cash flow. However, we can see from the statements that the decline was fully driven by investments in property, land, and equipment.

I believe that investors focusing on the short-term noise could miss out when Asian economies strengthen.

Adjusted earnings before interest and taxes were $431M higher year-over-year, with a 67-basis point margin improvement to 4.9%. Because I see this as a growth opportunity, it is better to look further out on revenue. We can see that revenue has more than doubled since 2020, and that is in a tougher economic environment for Asia. The company's cost of revenue has matched the sales growth, and that is hampering income, alongside the growth investments. However, this should improve with economic growth, and the company has potential to expand on its cloud operations.

The current financial dynamics are showing up in the valuation ratios with a very high price/earnings ratio of 196.83x on a GAAP forward basis. However, the price/sales ratio of 1.68x is much more appealing, despite a premium of 73% over the sector.

For comparison, Amazon (AMZN) has a P/S ratio of 3.47x, showing that there is strong upside potential for Coupang stock with a revival of Asian economic growth and a maturing profitability within the company.

Management has also noted scaling inefficiencies in Taiwan, which is common with early-stage market entries and should become more favorable with time.

Risks to the investment thesis

Coupang operates in a tough environment with more and more companies embracing e-commerce since the pandemic. Overseas expansion opportunities may be limited, and that means the company will have to bet on an economic revival in China and the broader region. Despite this, I believe the company is showing organic growth in flat markets, while they are also at the forefront of AI systems and efficiencies. The price/earnings ratios are high, but the price/sales shows that the company has some value in the current price.

Conclusion

Coupang began life as an 'Amazon Killer' but is paving a path for itself that can stay out of the crosshairs of the Amazon juggernaut. The company's specialization in bringing overseas brands into Korea and Taiwan should bode well in the future, especially if the Chinese economy strengthens. That could also happen at the expense of a slowdown in Western economies, and I believe that it makes a more compelling opportunity than Amazon's maturing model. Coupang has growth opportunities forming in areas like streaming and the cloud. The short-term finances should be viewed in that manner, and the recent cash flow dip was simply an investment into tangible assets.

data.title: Glaukos' Pipeline And High Valuation: A Risky Hold With Long-Term Upside
data.description:
Glaukos Corporation (NYSE:GKOS) stock price has been having a bad year with double-digit losses. However, over the past month, the stock has been showing some recovery signs, probably backed by an improved Q2 outturn. The company experienced an improvement in almost all its metrics, including revenue and gross margin, and displayed strong pipeline depth. That said, GKOS stock was flagged by Seeking Alpha last month as a stock at high risk of performing badly because it is 'overpriced and has negative EPS revisions when compared to other Health Care stocks'. The stock attracted a Sell rating on the Quant rating system, meaning it had massively underperformed the S&P 500. This rating still came after the release of Q2 results, which I feel were impressive. Despite the harsh Quant rating system flag, I'm of the opinion that GKOS stock might deserve some patience given its potential. I would rather HOLD Glaukos stock for now and watch its transition, as I believe it might in the near term.

Glaukos Corporation is a global ophthalmic medical technology and pharmaceutical company dedicated to innovative therapies for glaucoma, corneal disorders, and retinal diseases. The company pioneered Micro-Invasive Glaucoma Surgery (MIGS), launching its first device in 2012, and has since expanded into drug-delivery implants and corneal/retinal treatments. Headquartered in Aliso Viejo, California, with global operations, Glaukos completed its IPO in 2015, advancing eye care through novel, lasting treatment platforms.

How Glaukos Generates Revenue

Glaukos, being a medical tech company, focuses on eye diseases, namely glaucoma, corneal disorders, and retinal diseases.

Glaucoma is a disease that damages the optic nerve, often from high eye pressure. Corneal disorders are those problems affecting the clear front surface of the eye. Meanwhile, retinal diseases like macular degeneration affect the back of the eye.

For glaucoma treatments, Glaukos' flagship products are micro-invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS) devices like the iStent. These are tiny stents doctors implant during cataract surgery to help fluid drain from the eye. This lowers eye pressure, thereby slowing glaucoma damage. This revenue line is Glaukos' current core revenue driver.

Meanwhile, the company sells systems like iLink for corneal cross-linking. The systems are used in treating keratoconus, a disease that weakens and thins the cornea. This procedure strengthens the cornea using special drops and UV light, preventing worsening vision.

For retinal diseases, such projects are still in the pipeline and yet to be commercialise.

Brief Q2 Financial Highlights

Glaukos delivered a strong Q2 2025 performance, with record net sales of $124.1 million, representing a 30% increase year-over-year (YoY). This growth was lifted by its glaucoma segment, which saw net sales of $103.5 million, up 36% YoY.

Meanwhile, U.S. glaucoma sales were particularly robust, surging 45% to $72.3 million from the previous year. The company's non-GAAP gross margin improved to 83%, and as a result, the company raised its FY2025 sales guidance to between $480 million and $486 million.

I believe these results highlight solid operational execution and the successful launch of key products like iDose TR.

What's in Glaukos' Pipeline?

Glaukos is seeing beyond its current operations, with Epioxa being the most immediate in the pipeline. Epioxa is a non-invasive corneal cross-linking therapy for keratoconus treatment. The drug's new drug application (NDA) was accepted by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), while its Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) decision is due October 20, 2025. If approved, the drug could launch in 2026, replacing Photrexa while creating a better corneal revenue stream.

Meanwhile, the glaucoma segment remains anchored by iDose TR, a drug that is already commercial and delivering sustained drug release. The iDose Reimplantation NDA is under review, with a decision expected in January 2026. The good thing is that after the review, it'll create a recurring refill cycle from that year forward. That said, iDose TREX, with greater drug capacity, is now in Phase 2b/3 trials, with approval possible around 2027–2028.

Beyond iDose, Glaukos is pushing the PreserFlo MicroShunt, which is already approved in Europe, Canada, and Australia. The drug is in pivotal U.S. trials and is getting well-positioned for a potential FDA submission in the next few years and a possible U.S. approval around 2027.

For the longer term, the company's ILution platform and GLO-401 retinal therapy, currently in Phase 2, represent opportunities likely beyond 2028.

Key Risks to Watch

Product Transition: From Photrexa to Epioxa

Glaukos is expecting a PDUFA approval next month, October 2025, for Epioxa, which, if approved, will result in a commercial launch in 2026, and the drug could replace Photrexa.

Management has warned of a possible transient disruption as patients and providers might delay Photrexa procedures in anticipation of Epioxa. The Q2 earnings call has flagged a likely Q4 revenue impact as the market shifts. The bad part of this development is that Photrexa revenue was already under pressure after entering the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP), which reduced realized Photrexa revenue.

The major downside in my view is that if payer coverage is slow post-approval or if clinicians wait for post-launch experience, 2026 corneal revenue could be lower than modeled and will produce a quarter(s)-long revenue dip.

On the other hand, the glaucoma side with its iDose TR is already contributing strongly, yet its long-term revenue durability depends on the FDA’s decision on iDose Reimplantation, with a PDUFA date of January 28, 2026. I think a positive outcome would create a recurring revenue cycle from 2026 onward, while a denial would limit iDose to a one-time implant.

In parallel, iDose TREX is in Phase 2b/3 trials, and PreserFlo is progressing in U.S. studies, offering mid-decade upside. Together, these transitions represent near-term volatility but significant long-term growth opportunities. I think the bad side of things is how Glaukos' products are heavily dependent on approvals.

Stronger Competition

Glaukos is not alone in its space, and it looks like its competitors are bigger and have been performing and executing much better. Players like Regeneron and Roche are strong in the ophthalmology sector, particularly retina.

Regeneron is already generating nearly $6 billion annually through its blockbuster anti-Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor (VEGF), Eylea. This competitor carries the financial muscle to dominate research and development (R&D), marketing, and payer access.

On the other hand, Roche is a leader in innovation. The company's notable product is Vabysmo, which extends dosing to 16 weeks, coupled with its refillable Susvimo implant and broad retinal pipeline.

Sadly, Glaukos's retinal efforts have remained early-stage with its products, GLO-401 and iLution. To fight this kind of competition, Glaukos must apply precision in its operations by scaling surgeon training, winning reimbursement, and innovating in niches.

Overall, the space is not looking so friendly for Glaukos, and the company ought to work so hard.

Reimbursement Risk

It's important to note that doctors will only use Glaukos' devices and drugs on the condition that insurers reliably pay them back. Considering that most glaucoma patients are older, Medicare and Medicaid coverage in the U.S. is critical.

In glaucoma, coverage varies as some Medicare Administrative Contractors (MACs) reimburse iDoseTR, while others lag, slowing adoption. Nationally, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) has cut fees for older MIGS (Micro-Invasive Glaucoma Surgery) stents, pressuring surgeon economics and nudging shifts to newer options.

In corneal health, Photrexa revenues are reduced by Medicaid rebates, while Epioxa (FDA decision October 2025) still needs billing codes and payer approval, potentially delaying uptake.

Overall, I think that Glaukos’s reimbursement risk creates short-term bumps due to uneven coverage, and fee cuts slow adoption. Should Medicare and insurers gradually align, I foresee long-term visibility improving, thereby supporting stable, recurring revenues from new therapies.

Glaukos Cash Management

From the latest Q2 results, Glaukos appears financially capable of taking care of its ambitious pipeline without necessarily rushing back to raise cash.

I say so because it managed to deliver over $4 million in underlying operating cash during the quarter, showing it can almost cover itself despite heavy investment. The company's headline $25 million cash decline was tied to a one-off new headquarters (HQ) and the Mobius (MitoSol) acquisition, and that expense was not linked to day-to-day operations.

I also believe that with 83% gross margins, Glaukos generates a lot of cash from each sale, which helps offset its FY25 expected operating expense figure of around $460 million. Meanwhile, the company's debt is almost negligible at around 2% of capital, while liquidity is very strong with a 6.5x current ratio. This means that Glaukos can comfortably pay its near-term obligations.

This is despite the fact that the company posted losses in Q2 (EPS -0.24) and that it has accumulated deficits from years of R&D. Such a trend is not out of bounds but a common phenomenon, especially for medtech innovators.

I still believe that Glaukos, with disciplined spending, low debt, and strong gross margins, can fund its near- and mid-term projects (Epioxa, iDose Reimplantation, PreserFlo) from internal resources while retaining flexibility for later-stage retinal bets.

High Risk of Performing Badly

As I highlighted earlier, Glaukos' stock is looking overvalued on Seeking Alpha quant screens, a perception I partly share as well. That said, I would also think that the stock might deserve the benefit of the doubt, given its pipeline and market position.

Considering that iDoseTR is ramping quickly and should Epioxa’s FDA decision in October 2025 be positive, it could open a new corneal revenue stream in 2026. With 83% gross margins, minimal debt, and near cash-flow breakeven, Glaukos can self-fund innovation.

Additionally, considering that ophthalmology markets like glaucoma and keratoconus are chronic and growing, they could offer lasting growth momentum. My other sense thinks that if execution stays on track, today’s premium multiples could normalize as revenues inflect in 2026–2028.

Valuation

Glaukos' forward Price/Sales ratio of 10.36 suggests the market has high expectations for its future revenue growth. This is significantly higher than both the industry average of 3.92 and its competitor Sight Sciences's ratio of 2.39. I believe this high valuation multiple indicates that investors are willing to pay a premium for Glaukos' stock, likely due to the promising pipeline products like iDose TR and Epioxa. While this points to strong future growth potential, it's important also to note it exposes the stock to risk if these pipeline products face delays or do not achieve the expected market success. On the other hand, the much lower Price/Sales ratio for Sight Sciences might suggest it is undervalued or that the market perceives it to have less growth potential compared to Glaukos.

Conclusion

Based on the above factors affecting Glaukos, I think a HOLD rating is a prudent conclusion. While the company's impressive Q2 performance and robust product pipeline are signaling strong long-term growth potential, significant near-term risks warrant caution. Meanwhile, the high valuation, potential revenue disruption from the Photrexa to Epioxa transition, and intense competition from larger players like Regeneron and Roche create volatility. Therefore, I would suggest that waiting for clearer signals on regulatory approvals and market adoption is the most reasonable strategy at this time.

data.title: CRE: 2 Sectors Becoming Quite Concerning
data.description:
Today, I return to the deteriorating commercial real estate markets. While not nearly as large as the residential real estate market which is currently becoming toxic, it is an important part of the economy.

Outside of the Industrial property sector, delinquency rates on CRE loans have become concerning. While Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities or CMBS delinquency rates on both Retail and Lodging properties have moved up to around 6.5%, the two areas investors should have on their radar are the rapidly deteriorating Multifamily and Office sectors.

Let's start with Multifamily where CMBS delinquencies have more than doubled to 6.86% over the past 12 months. This delinquency rate does not capture loans that have been modified; it should be noted. Multifamily loans represent approximately $2.2 trillion of roughly $4.8 trillion of CRE debt outstanding.

The Multifamily space faces issues on multiple fronts right now. First, a good chunk of this debt was taken on during the ZIRP period when interest rates were much lower. Given commercial real estate loans typically run five to seven years, this debt load will have to be refinanced at significantly higher rates in the quarters and years ahead.

Some of this debt was funneled into the huge increase in apartments in the U.S. that have come online in recent years especially in areas that had benefited from the intrastate migration following Covid and its aftermath like Florida, Texas, and Tennessee. This resulted in 592,000 apartments opening in 2024. That was the best level since 1974, when the U.S. population was much smaller.

This obviously has contributed to a significant new rental supply becoming available. On the demand side, some 1.6 million net migrants have left the country in the first seven months of 2025. Notably, most are from self-deportation. With the significant increases for immigration enforcement that were incorporated into the recently passed 2026 federal budget, this is a trend that will likely continue in the coming quarters and years.

The combination of a large amount of supply coming on in recent quarters with falling demand on the margins is starting to show up in slowing rental growth (above). Importantly, this does not capture the increasing use of incentives (e.g., two months free on a 14-month lease) being utilized to address higher vacancies.

Unfortunately, the costs for property taxes and insurance are still increasing at a solid clip across much of the nation as are maintenance costs. You can see these pressures within the second quarter results from giant apartment REIT AvalonBay Communities, Inc. (AVB) where same-property revenue rose less than same-property expenses in the second quarter. It is little wonder the stock has moved down this year despite the rally in the overall market as well as the recent fall in interest rates.

Moving over to the developing disaster known as the Office sector. This accounts for approximately $1.3 trillion of the overall $4.8 trillion of CRE debt outstanding. CMBS delinquency rates have moved from under two percent in 2022 before the Federal Reserve embarked on the most aggressive monetary policy since the days of Paul Volcker, to nearly 11.7% in August. And again, this does not include loan modifications or other 'extend and pretend' measures taken by lenders.

Everything continues to work against this area of the commercial real estate sector. Office values have plunged in recent years in markets such as Los Angeles, Baltimore, and Chicago. Myriad buildings sport negative equity values with the collapse of values in many regions of the country. The national office vacancy rate reached 19.8% by the 2024 year-end. This was up 150bps on a year-over-year basis despite the lowest square footage of new office property coming online in 2024 since 2013.

The return to office push by major corporations like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) stalled out overall in early 2023. There have been a few exceptions like NYC, which has seen office vacancy rates fall by solid margins. However, as I noted in an article earlier this week, the likelihood of Mamdani winning the mayoral election in November could present a new headwind on that front.

Nor can most of these properties be converted to other uses like residential real estate. The cost and structural changes needed to convert office buildings to condos only make financial sense for a small percentage of buildings.

Given these factors, I expect delinquency rates to continue to climb in this sector. Eventually, extend and pretend measures will end, and lenders will increasingly start to bite the bullet. This will be especially true if the country enters a recession. This will result in an uptick in foreclosures and higher credit losses for lenders.

So, who owns this debt? Commercial banks and thrifts own just less than 40%, Agency and GSEs like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac hold just over 20% of these obligations, and life insurance companies hold just over 15%. Of note, Fannie Mae added just under $950 million to their credit loss provisions in their most recent quarter. This could complicate the potential efforts to bring it and Freddie Mac public again after government ownership since 2008.

In summary, these two parts of the commercial real estate market continue to deteriorate. While the approximate $3.5 trillion of debt obligations pales against the approximate $13 trillion in residential mortgage debt outstanding, it is still a considerable amount. More importantly, it is hard to see how this will not be increasing headwind to the U.S. economy in the quarters ahead.

data.title: Madison Square Garden Sports: Knicks And Rangers On Wall Street
data.description:
MSGS: two franchises, many constraints

If you follow the NBA or the NHL, you will certainly recognize the New York Knicks and the New York Rangers. What you may not know is that both franchises operate under the same company: Madison Square Garden Sports (NYSE:MSGS).

It is important to note that MSGS does not own the iconic arena itself. Instead, it operates under a long-term license with MSG Entertainment to use Madison Square Garden, and under a contract with MSG Networks for the local distribution of games played in New York. That means the company’s revenue mix is ultimately shaped by matchday income, sponsorship agreements, and media rights.

The problem, in my view, is that not everything depends on management execution. On the media side, for instance, the NBA has just signed a record-breaking national deal that will increase guaranteed payouts to teams starting in 2026. However, MSG Networks has had to restructure its debt and renegotiate payments lower, which cut the fee received by the Knicks and Rangers by 23% in a contract that runs until 2029.

Then there is the sporting and financial side, which I find particularly hard to project when margins depend on what happens either on the court or in the league offices. The NBA sets a salary cap for teams, and a luxury tax for those who exceed it. This year, the Knicks went over and will have to pay more than $29M as a result. On top of that, another $30M comes from revenue sharing. That is $60M hat will be redistributed to smaller franchises in order to keep the league balanced.

So, even with the Garden packed and global sponsorship contracts being signed, a record season like the last one can still end up with only marginal operating profit. That is why the most reasonable way I see to value MSGS is either through EV/Sales, which reflects how similar transactions have been priced, or through a sum-of-the-parts approach, since two franchises of this caliber may hold hidden value that the market has yet to fully recognize.

Knicks and Rangers set records, the income statement does not

The Dolan family has controlled MSGS since around 2010, and that grip was reinforced in 2020 after the separation of MSG Entertainment. Thanks to the Class B shares, they hold 70% of the voting power with less than 5% of the economic capital. In practice, that means nearly two decades of taking the strategic decisions that shape both the Knicks and the Rangers.

In the latest report, the company posted a historic revenue record above $1B, so it is worth breaking down the mix to see which verticals are contributing each dollar and to what extent.

The largest share comes from matchday, which accounts for almost a third of total revenues. Here we find ticket sales, season passes, and hospitality. With the Garden running at nearly 100% average occupancy and Knicks season tickets renewing at over 90%, there is enough evidence to confirm the strong momentum of the show.

The sponsorship vertical is also in good shape, especially after management announced during the last call the signing of a global contract that will take this line to record levels as well.

Closing out the mix is media, which in theory should be the most stable, yet has become the source of uncertainty. From this season onward, the NBA has signed an eleven-year deal with Disney, ESPN, NBC, and Amazon to distribute roughly $7B annually among the franchises. This is a notable jump compared with the $24B spread over nine years under the prior agreement.

The “but” comes from whether the renegotiation with MSG Networks can really offset the NBA deal. Previously, the Knicks and Rangers received close to $180M in local rights, but after the restructuring that figure has dropped by 23% to $138M. The effect was clear in Q3 FY25, revenues were down by $18.6M year-on-year, and annualized that translates into a hit of roughly $40M.

The paradox is evident. Knicks posting record revenues, sponsorships and renewals, Rangers filling the Garden, and yet operating profit ends up at just $38M after bringing in $1B.

With these numbers, I can only conclude that what happens on the court will be what sets MSGS’s path for the next year. The media side is clearly dragging results down, leaving everything in the hands of whether the Knicks and Rangers manage another deep playoff run.

And without further catalysts in sight, it becomes very hard to project a valuation on the assumption that the Knicks can go all the way this year.

From Madison to Wall Street: scenarios for MSGS

With the cut in MSG Networks, the luxury tax for exceeding the salary cap, and revenue sharing as one of the highest–earning franchises, margins are practically wiped out. That is why my valuation hypothesis is based either on EV/Sales or on a SOTP approach.

In terms of SOTP, according to Sportico the New York Knicks are valued at around $8B, and the Rangers at about $3.5B. Since this is not a private transaction we still have to apply some adjustments. From this $11B figure we need to subtract the net debt of about $1B.

And finally, I apply a 20% discount, because it is not the same to buy 100% of the business as it is to hold a minority stake, with no control. With these calculations, the net asset value would be around $7.8B, and dividing it between roughly 23M shares gives us a range of $330 to $340 per share.

The problem is that this NAV is just that, theoretical. Today MSGS trades at 5.7x EV/Sales FWD and at more than 50x P/FCF, multiples that look too demanding for an operating profit of only $38M. In private transactions we have seen sales around 12x EBITDA, such as the Suns in 2023, or around 10x EBITDA in the case of the Hornets.

In the NHL, Senators and Penguins have also traded in that 10–12x band. If we compare with football clubs like Manchester United (MANU) or Juventus (OTCPK:JVTSF), they trade at 3–4x EV/Sales, but their businesses are structurally weaker. For MSGS, EV/Sales seems reasonable, but a P/FCF of 20x would be the limit of what makes sense.

Breaking this down into scenarios makes it easier to understand. In a base year, with the Knicks reaching the second round and the Rangers making the playoffs, normalized FCF would be around $100M, which anchors the share price at $230. In a bear case, without a Knicks deep run and with local media revenues down, FCF would fall to $80–85M, justifying a range of $190–200. And in an optimistic case, with the Knicks in the Conference Finals and the Rangers alongside them, FCF would rise to $130–140M, which points to $250–260.

  • Deep run: revenues 1.1B, FCF 130–140M, multiple 6x EV/Sales FWD = $250 per share
  • Standard season: revenues 1.05B, FCF 90–100M, multiple 5.5x EV/Sales FWD = $230 per share
  • No playoffs: revenues 1B, FCF 80–85M, multiple 5x EV/Sales FWD = $180 per share

My base case is at least a standard season, and that anchors the share price at $230. There is not an excessive upside at these levels, and too many conditions to meet, but I lean toward a buy with a minimum target of $230 per share.

The market already draws its range: $190–$230

I always like to check the technical side to contrast my fundamental hypotheses, and this time was no different. What I see is that the price has been trapped since 2020 in a lateral range between $140 and $200, even though in 2022 a promising uptrend seemed to start.

When I cross this picture with what the options market is telling me for November 2025, I keep seeing that same lateral range, with puts concentrating around $190 and calls accumulating around $230. This confirms the hypothesis I have already set out, that the $230 per share scenario is what the market is pricing in unless a catalyst appears, whether a share buyback, a minority sale, or another deep run in the playoffs like last season.

I therefore reaffirm my view that at current prices around $200, with an EV/Sales FWD of 5.7x and a P/FCF of 50x, the multiples are too demanding to push the stock sustainably beyond $230 without that extra catalyst.

The risks that keep the discount

Now then, what could happen to invalidate my $230 per share scenario? I start from the assumption that the Knicks and the Rangers will perform in a similar way to last season, that the new national contract signed by the NBA will cover part of the hole left by MSG Network, and that this will leave the company with a stable FCF of around $100M. But I also see some risks that could easily derail that base case.

The audiovisual side is the one that worries me the most, because local media rights have already fallen 23%, and until 2029 that pressure will remain unless an alternative model appears once the current agreement ends.

Then we have the cost structure imposed by the leagues, both NBA and NHL. The Knicks exceed the salary cap almost every year, and as one of the top earners, they end up paying luxury tax and revenue sharing that instead of going down, could even rise further, since this is something MSGS cannot fully control.

And finally, the sports factor. What happens on the court inevitably shapes the operating result, and if the teams do not advance far enough in the competitions, the impact will be immediate in matchday and sponsorship revenues.

On top of this, there do not seem to be clear catalysts to the upside, beyond a strong season on the sporting front.

Buy, but with too many conditions

I keep a Buy rating on MSGS.

My conclusion is that despite the structural problems around local media rights, and the league rules on luxury tax and revenue sharing, MSGS still owns scarce and valuable sports assets.

With a NAV for the Knicks and Rangers that would easily exceed $300 even after applying a reasonable discount, there is still margin at current levels.

Of course, this is not a decision you can take just by looking at the accounting snapshot, because a P/FCF of 50x would be an automatic sell in other companies. But an EV/Sales FWD of 5.7x is not unreasonable for MSGS.

On top of that, the new NBA contract and the fact that MSGS is reporting record revenues lead me to this conclusion. I expect revenue stability, even with all the uncertainty around the business.

It will remain conditioned by what happens on the court, and nothing guarantees that the teams will deliver the results needed this season to push the stock to $230. But on a 12-month view, I do see it as feasible if they can repeat a season similar to the last one.

data.title: Alliance Entertainment: Margins, Cash, And More Keep Me Bullish
data.description:
Thesis

I initiated coverage of Alliance Entertainment Holding Corporation (NASDAQ:AENT) as a Buy several months ago. This was probably going against the consensus for this microcap by arguing the market was "missing" a turnaround driven by better operations, automation, cleaner inventory, a growing set of exclusive licensing deals, and a very unique, let’s call it a very “old school” business model. Meanwhile, cash flow was improving, leverage was coming down, and the valuation implied very low expectations.

So far, so good. The market has backed up my thesis and AENT has doubled in price (since my buy call), while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up roughly 11%.

A quick and easy conclusion here would be that the call has played out and it's time to harvest. Maybe. In this update I’ll examine the latest earnings report, highlight what changed in the business and the balance sheet, and update my rating.

Alliance Entertainment: Margins Lead Despite Sales Dip

Overall, things are looking positive for Alliance’s product mix. Holiday shopping is expected to grow at a moderate pace, with online sales leading the way. The U.S. has also extended Section 301 tariff exclusions on some goods imported from China until late November. According to management, tariffs don’t really affect music and video products much, but they do impact collectibles. Even so, Alliance has been able to pass along the higher costs to customers without hurting demand.

In this latest Q4, Alliance brought in $227.8 million in revenue, which was down 3.9% from the same time last year. Even though sales dipped, the types of products sold (the mix) and how well the company executed still made a big difference. Gross profit jumped 34% to $36.0 million. On top of that, the gross margin improved to 15.8%, up from 11.4% a year earlier. This improvement came from selling more higher-margin products and from efficiency gains thanks to automation.

Profitability improved as well. Net income came in at $5.8 million, which works out to $0.11 per diluted share, more than double the $0.05 per share earned a year ago. Adjusted EBITDA also climbed sharply to $12.2 million, up from just $2.1 million last year.

The company also became more efficient. Distribution and fulfillment costs dropped by about 1 percentage point as a share of revenue, thanks to its automation hub in Kentucky and a smaller footprint in Minnesota.

The company earned a little less money in total sales this year compared to last. They brought in $1.06 billion, while the year before they had $1.10 billion. And even though those sales dipped slightly, Alliance actually kept more money from what it sold. That’s because its gross profit went up to $132.9 million. This made its gross margin rise from 11.7% to 12.5%.

That improvement in efficiency carried over into earnings. Net income came in at $15.1 million. On a per-share basis, that’s $0.30, which is more than 3x what it was the previous year at $0.09. The EBITDA figure jumped by 51%, reaching $36.5 million.

Balance Sheet Gains and Structural Leverage

Alliance brought in $26.8 million in cash from its day-to-day business activities. Its regular costs, or operating expenses, dropped by a little more than 10%, and the money it had to pay in interest went down by almost 14%. It also cut back the amount it owed on its revolving credit line by 22%.

The CFO explained that the firm's use of that aforementioned "automation" and the decision to combine warehouses are now showing up in its profit and loss statement. The good news is that because of those changes, they can handle more business without seeing costs rise at the same pace. That’s called operating leverage, and it’s exactly what a business hopes to have when it’s about to enter its busiest time of year.

Management also explained on the conference call that its exclusive partnerships added more than $350 million in sales, which now makes up over one-third of its total revenue. A big example is the deal with Paramount for home entertainment, which is projected to keep growing through calendar year 2025 and covers DVD, Blu-ray, Ultra HD, and collector editions. Yes, it sounds very dated, that's the "old school" I referred to earlier. Even so, clearly, we're talking about tangible products, sans streaming fees and so forth, bought and paid for, one and done, and there's a niche group of consumers who still gobble these products up.

The company has also strengthened its spot in retail. Here's another factor why I bought this company last time since they were already in bed with them: in August, Walmart Inc. (WMT) chose them to act as the adviser for its video category. That means Alliance helps coordinate how studios and Walmart plan together, making sure the right products get to the right shelves.

The company’s DTC fulfillment business grew to 37% of total revenue, up from 36%. Keep in mind, this part of the business doesn’t require heavy investments in equipment or property, so it’s considered “capital-light.” It also allows the company to offer a wider range of products, which is called SKU breadth, all while improving profit margins.

Inside the company’s operations, Alliance is adding new tech on top of that existing automation. Right now, 250 employees are using Microsoft Corporation's (MSFT) Copilot, the engineering team is working with GitHub, and a rollout of HubSpot, Inc. (HUBS) is underway. And when it comes to handling money, I think it's a great call that management's choosing to reinvest cash back into the business and pursue disciplined M&A. So don't expect any kind of stock buybacks in the near future.

Finally, management wrapped up by saying that Q4 showed a gross margin of 15.8% and an adjusted EBITDA margin above 5%. They believe those levels can be maintained as a solid starting point for fiscal 2026. In the near term, demand should get an extra boost in the second quarter thanks to a major music release coming in October, followed by a busier schedule of releases heading into the holiday season.

Alliance Entertainment’s Valuation

Right now, the AENT's forward P/E ratio is 7.58. That’s very low.

Its blended P/E is higher at 16.07 (see above).

Also, according to FAST Graphs, the fair value P/E should be around 19.89. If that fair value turns out to be right, assuming you buy today and the company’s earnings turn out the way analysts expect, the return could be about 20% per year.

You saw the revenue numbers earlier. They are massive for a micro-cap. The main reason to think this stock could go up isn’t because the company is suddenly going to sell a lot more stuff. It’s because they’re expected to make a lot more profit on what they already sell.

Wall Street analysts think EPS will rise by 139% next year. If the company can keep its gross profit margin in the mid-teens (about 14–16%) and keep its adjusted EBITDA margin above 5%, then the current low stock price looks too pessimistic. In that case, the stock hitting about $7.25 to $7.50 wouldn’t be a big stretch, and reaching $10 would just take good old-fashioned steady execution, not a miracle.

As far as my main concern in the risk category goes, this is a very low-margin distributor, so even if profits rise, revenue can still slip. The EPS growth path might very well be disrupted if the sales mix swings in the wrong direction, or, and in my opinion, this is the most significant issue, if the holiday season turns out to be far less profitable than anticipated.

Final Takeaway

The core thesis appears to be intact and on track, so I'm sticking with my buy rating. Gross margin is holding in the mid-teens (around 14–16%), and adjusted EBITDA margin is holding above 5%. Those levels are being supported by things like more automation, better control of inventory, and a growing lineup of exclusive products, especially from Paramount. On top of that, the company is bringing in more cash, paying down debt, and lowering leverage. Add in Walmart naming them as a category adviser and the fact that their DTC business doesn’t require a lot of capital, and the setup still looks strong.

data.title: Forget Chatbots: Atlassian's Real AI Play Is The Browser
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Atlassian Corporation (NASDAQ:TEAM) is a stock to consider in your portfolio not as a gamble, but as a long-term investment. I am optimistic about the stock for three main reasons. First, the company has recorded solid growth (revenues up 20% per year). Second, margins are already close to 25% (which is the company's target) and free cash flow exceeds $1.4 billion. Finally, there is a very interesting strategic bet, namely to transform the browser into a true artificial intelligence hub for digital work, opening up new prospects for development.

The company has a huge customer base, with a mix of attractively priced products (Jira, Confluence, JSM, Loom), a re-accelerating cloud business, and rapidly growing AI adoption. At these multiples, there is still room to grow, especially if it maintains growth of around 20% and cost discipline. The recent acquisition of The Browser Company (Dia/Arc) puts Atlassian in a position to integrate AI + browser into the platform to create a single point of access to work (not just from the app). If it works, it reduces the need for separate chatbots and increases lock-in (data and flows remain “in-house”).

This combination of execution, cash flow, and innovation creates the conditions for the stock to have an excellent risk/return profile, with a potential rerating that the market has not yet priced in and that may prove to be the real call option. For me, at these levels, it is a Strong Buy.

Company Overview

I quote verbatim from the company website:

Atlassian is a recognized leader in software development, work management, and enterprise service management. The company enables companies to connect their business and technology teams with an AI-powered work system that increases productivity at scale. Atlassian's collaboration software powers over 80% of the Fortune 500 and over 300,000 customers worldwide, including NASA, Rivian, Deutsche Bank, United Airlines, and Bosch, who rely on our solutions to advance their work.

For those unfamiliar with the company, Atlassian is a global software company specializing in collaboration and work management. Its main products include Jira, used by development teams to plan and monitor projects, and Confluence, a sort of “company wiki” where teams from any company can write, share, and organize information. Atlassian has taken advantage of this by building a veritable “viral ecosystem” around it. In fact, its tools often start with technical teams and then spread throughout the organization, up to the enterprise level.

Another important business area for the company is the Marketplace, which offers over 8,000 apps and generates over $5 billion in cumulative sales, making the Atlassian cloud increasingly integrated and difficult to replace with other competitors. Added to this is the Teamwork Collection, a package that includes core products at a lower price than purchasing them individually. Many companies are adopting it, and the result is an increase in average spending of about 20%. It's a bit like when a pub sells you a pint of beer with chips; it's true that you pay more, but you get everything you need right away, and it's good for the pub because it makes more money from each customer.

In other words, Atlassian is growing not only by adding new customers but above all by constantly increasing the spending of companies that already use its products, making them dependent on its products. Excellent execution!

Earnings Season – Q4 FY25

A month ago, Atlassian presented its quarterly results and, despite weak performance on the stock market, the figures remain solid. Revenues rose to $1.38 billion (+22% compared to a year ago), with Cloud growing 26% to over $928 million. For me, this is a clear sign: even in a context of IT budget cuts, Atlassian is managing to gain more ground among large enterprise customers.

Another aspect that shows how the company is growing at a steady and interesting pace is its margins. In fact, non-GAAP operating margin rose to 24% (from 20% a year ago) and quarterly free cash flow reached $360 million (26% of revenue). I believe this is an aspect that should not be underestimated because, while the market very often looks solely and exclusively at growth in general, Atlassian also has a level of financial discipline that is not often seen.

If we look at the whole year, revenues amounted to $5.2 billion (+20%) and over $1.4 billion in free cash flow, with margins at 27%. So we are talking about a mature company that finances its own innovation with abundant liquidity at its disposal, rather than a “bet on AI”!

At the end of June, the company had 2.3 million monthly active users, an increase of 50% in a single quarter. It is obvious that monetization will come later, but when a company reaches millions of MAUs in such a short time, it means that it has already found its target product-market.

The forecasts for fiscal year 2026 remain cautious, and I consider this an advantage because, in my opinion, it is better to be cautious and then surprise on the upside than to make overly optimistic promises and then have to lower estimates and disappoint investors. In any case, according to estimates, the company expects an 18% increase in total revenue, a 21% increase in cloud revenue, and margins around 24%. I interpret this as a conservative “waterline,” typical of companies that prefer to surprise on the upside in the future.

Valuation Supporting the Rating

At current price levels, Atlassian trades at around 7x forward EV/Sales and 22.5x forward EV/FCF, which on paper are not “discounted” multiples, and so far we all agree.

Nevertheless, as we highlighted earlier, we must remember that the company is growing steadily at a rate of around 20% per year, generating over $1.4 billion in annual free cash flow with margins already close to 25%, so it can easily finance all innovations directly with the liquidity it generates, without the risk of capital increases and therefore dilution for shareholders.

To estimate the target price, I chose a direct and easy-to-understand approach, without too many frills. I take as a reference multiples consistent with software vendor companies that grow around 20% and maintain margins in the 25% range. In practice, 11x EV/Sales and 35x EV/FCF. These are not invented multiples, but the range that the market tends to recognize for those who manage to combine growth, profitability, and cash generation of this type.

Let's start with what we know, namely that Atlassian's revenues are expected to reach $6.16 billion in FY26 and $7.36 billion in FY27 (Bloomberg estimates). Applying 11x EV/Sales, the implied value of the company ranges from $68 billion to $81 billion. On the cash side, estimates indicate approximately $2.1 billion in FCF in FY26 and nearly $3 billion in FY27, which multiplied by 35x leads to an even wider range, between $74 billion and $104 billion in EV.

Usually, when I analyze stocks, I don't like to be overly optimistic and then be surprised negatively. I prefer to be cautious and consider the midpoint, which is $85-90 billion in EV. Today, Atlassian is worth $46.9 billion, which means there is room for an increase of almost double. In short, the correct share price is between $315 and $330, an increase of about 90% from current levels. I'm talking about concrete numbers related to growth trajectory and the ability to monetize AI with margins close to management's target.

Conclusion

The main risk for Atlassian is its ability to execute, given that the “forced” migration from Data Center to Cloud may cause complications with legacy customers. I cannot fail to mention the complications associated with internal tensions related to the reorganization and, in general, to valuations that are objectively above the industry average.

I see more positives than negatives, and therefore more opportunities than risks at the moment, such as the AI browser that could become the new gateway to digital work, or the partnership with Google Cloud that reduces dependence on AWS, or the AI model that is already ready to generate revenue through pay-as-you-go credits.

The risk/return profile is therefore favorable for everything we have discussed so far. There are obviously risks, but they are limited and well-balanced by a solid financial structure and margins close to 25%. The negative scenario would imply more moderate but sustainable growth, while the positive scenario could justify a rise to the $315-330 per share range, almost double the current value.

data.description:
Kepler Is Growing, But Valuation Expectations Are Excessive

Kepler Group Limited (KPL) has filed to raise $7 million in an IPO of its ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1 registration information.

KPL provides insurance brokerage and ancillary services in Hong Kong.

However, the company faces numerous risks, falling net results, and has excessive valuation assumptions, so my outlook on the KPL IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Kepler Do?

Hong Kong, PRC-based Kepler Group Limited was founded to offer a variety of insurance products and services as a broker representing major insurance companies.

Management is led by founder, chairman, and CEO Mr. Kwok Yu Hin, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2016 and was previously at Convoy Financial Services Limited as associate director and has nearly 20 years of experience in the insurance industry.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Insurance products

  • Immigration services

  • Software upgrade and maintenance

Kepler has booked a fair market value investment of $218,802 as of March 31, 2025, from investors, including Zhu Meizhen and others.

The firm’s revenue comes primarily from insurance brokerage commissions, as the pie charts show here:

Approximately 10% for recruitment of personals for the expansion of operations; and

The balance to fund working capital and for other general corporate purposes.

Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have moved higher as revenue has increased, and its Administrative efficiency multiple was only 0.8x.

KPL’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent lightly on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The firm’s growth strategy includes possible expansion into other Asian countries and growing its service offerings.

The market opportunity for providing insurance services in Hong Kong is large and expected to grow at a moderate rate of growth in the coming years.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘foreign private issuer’ and ‘emerging growth company’ status, which enables management to disclose less information to shareholders.

Also, the firm has high customer credit concentration risk and related party transactions, which can be viewed in more detail here.

As to valuation, Kepler is seeking an EV/EBITDA multiple of 71x versus an industry average of between 4x and 6x in Q3 2024.

Most agencies also have an EV/Revenue multiple of between 2x and 3x versus Kepler’s 4.9x expectation.

Given the many risks the company faces, its falling net results, and excessive valuation assumptions, my outlook on the KPL IPO is to sell (avoid it).
